# AGENTS.md

This file is the authoritative source of architectural and behavioral commitments the AI agent applies when writing or modifying code in this project. Each block below states a single directive the agent follows at code-author time.

These are prose-enforcement rules. The agent reads and respects them by judgment, not by lint or compiler check. They were adopted from the camerata principle library by the project's architect. The rules represent committed choices, not suggestions.

The companion file CONVENTIONS.md holds this project's structured and mechanical conventions, organized by citable rule ID. The agent reads both files when starting work and cites the rule IDs from CONVENTIONS.md when referencing them in commits or PR descriptions.

Do not edit this file by hand. To change the rules, edit the project's principle selection and regenerate via camerata.

---

## Universal

### ARCH-EXPAND-CONTRACT-1 — Expand-then-contract for breaking schema changes
Split breaking changes (drop/rename/NOT-NULL) across releases: expand (add new, write both), migrate reads, then contract (drop old). Migrations are schema-only; never seed user data in a migration.

### ORCH-BUDGET-MONITOR-1 — Budget self-monitoring
Track spend in a rolling window against a safety threshold, with per-session caps, and self-check periodically during a run. A wrapper enforces hard limits before invoking the model.

### ORCH-CLEAR-WINNER-1 — The clear-winner test — decide vs. stop
Before stopping for a human, run the test. (a) Is there a documented convention? Apply it, cite it, continue. (b) Does a guiding principle (e.g. robustness) break the tie? Apply the winner, document it, continue. Stop only if neither resolves it.

### ORCH-CONFLICTING-ROBUSTNESS-1 — Conflicting robustness is a real tradeoff, not a clear winner
When options are each robust against different failures (rigid constraints vs. bad data; flexibility vs. changing requirements), name both failure modes and pause to escalate the decision to a human reviewer. That is a genuine tradeoff, not a clear winner. Let the human pick which failure mode their context cares about more.

### ORCH-CONTEXT-OVERRIDE-1 — Rule overrides driven by project context require explicit human sign-off
When the agent identifies that applying a documented rule would produce wrong-shaped output for the project, the agent pauses and escalates the override to a human reviewer before either updating the rule or producing any artifact that diverges from it. Wrong-shaped output here means context-fit failures such as: the rule's stance conflicts with the project's positioning, a downstream constraint defeats the rule's rationale, an ecosystem convention diverges from the rule's stance, or similar misalignments between the rule's assumptions and the project's actual context. The escalation cites the rule by identifier and proposes the override. The agent resumes only after explicit sign-off. Silent override of a documented rule is forbidden.

### ORCH-MODEL-TIERING-1 — Model tiering — cheap for bulk, top-tier for decisions
Run high-volume mechanical work (applying documented rules, repetitive porting) on a cheaper model; reserve the top model for genuinely new decisions and one-way-door validation.

### ORCH-NO-NATURAL-BREAK-1 — Never stop on a "natural break"
A completed chunk, a milestone, or a periodic self-check is NOT a valid stop. The only valid exits are: budget/time cap, a real escalation trigger, queue genuinely empty, or unrecoverable error. Don't wrap early with budget unspent.

### ORCH-NOVELTY-1 — Novelty downgrades authority
A choice with no clear analog in existing conventions is treated as contested; lack of precedent lowers confidence even when the winner feels obvious. Pause and escalate the decision to a human reviewer rather than auto-applying the obvious-feeling answer.

### ORCH-ONE-WAY-DOOR-1 — One-way-door routing — blast radius gates autonomy
Even with a clear winner, STOP and escalate the decision to a human reviewer if the call is hard to reverse: it shapes the data model, a public/API contract, or a pattern that gets copied. Two-way doors (local, encapsulated, trivially refactored) you may auto-decide. Structural/topology changes (new/moved crate or module boundary, changed public trait/API surface) always pause for a human, even with a clear winner: reversibility decays as downstream code adopts the new shape. Decaying reversibility, not file count, is the real property. ("Routing" in the title refers to where the decision goes: to the AI to auto-apply, or to a human to review, based on the blast radius of getting it wrong.)

### ORCH-OUTPUT-DIGEST-1 — Output discipline — local-mostly + a single consolidated digest
Autonomous routines produce local artifacts (commits, state files) and write to a shared inbox. A single daily consolidator ranks all routines' output by priority and sends one digest, rather than each routine pinging directly. Critical items break through this cadence and notify immediately.

### ORCH-PRECISION-RECALL-1 — Autonomous detection defaults to recall, precision-tuning is a per-signal opt-in
Detection signals in autonomous routines (tech-debt sniffers, anomaly flags, drift detectors, anything that gates the routine on a judgment call) are tuned broadly. Anything that could plausibly be a real issue triggers a pause, including borderline cases that turn out to be false positives. The cost of stopping to investigate a false positive is small and recoverable. The cost of letting a real issue slip through silently is unbounded. Default posture: catch too much, not too little.

### ORCH-TIERED-ESCALATION-1 — Tiered escalation — soft-flag by default, hard-pause only for guard areas
Flagged items are soft-flagged (logged, routine continues on other work). Hard-pause (halt until a human clears it) applies only to sensitive hard-guard areas (auth, payments, infra, CI) and to overflow conditions when the review queue passes a threshold.

### ORCH-TRAINING-CUTOFF-1 — Training-data-limited decisions route to human review by category, not by confidence
When a decision depends on the current state of something the agent cannot directly observe from inside the project (third-party dependency versions, framework or vendor API shapes, latest model identifiers, current security-advisory state, anything whose ground truth ships outside the project's own source tree and moves faster than the agent's training cutoff), the agent does not decide unilaterally. It surfaces the question to a human reviewer along with the candidate answer and the citation it would use. The trigger is the CATEGORY of question, not the agent's subjective confidence: confidence calibrated against a stale training snapshot is the failure mode the rule exists to prevent. The category is visible, shipped version surfaces with an external ground truth. The rule does NOT cover stable, self-correcting surfaces like the host language's edition or backwards-compatible compiler features, where the project's own declared version anchors the answer.

### SPIRIT-FILE-SIZE-1 — Files are sized for human reading, not for AI write-cost
Hand-maintained files are split at sizes a human can navigate without losing their place. AI can generate and read a 2000-line file at no cost; every human who reviews, debugs, or maintains it pays full attention price. Auto-generated files (schema dumps, generated entity files, ORM-emitted models, lockfiles) are exempt: a tool maintains them, not a person.

### SPIRIT-OPTIMIZE-1 — Optimization by default
Bake performant patterns (parallel async, caching, indexing, no N+1) into conventions from day one, as the default shape of new code, not a reactive fix after profiling.

### SPIRIT-ROBUSTNESS-1 — Robustness over terseness, but buy complexity only against a named threat
When two correct implementations differ, robustness (not verbosity) is the deciding axis. Choose the more explicit version. AI bears the boilerplate cost, and explicitness gives future AI more context to debug and extend. When the more robust option is also more complex (not just more explicit), name the specific failure mode the complexity is buying you protection against. Do not harden throwaway, internal, or fully-encapsulated code where nothing is at stake.

## Rust

### RUST-DOMAIN-2 — Newtype IDs (every ID is a wrapper, never a bare Uuid)
UserId(Uuid), OrganizationId(Uuid); bare Uuid only at persistence and HTTP boundaries.

_Applies to:_ `**/*.rs`

### RUST-DOMAIN-5 — Async all the way down
I/O functions, repo traits, service methods, and handlers are async; no block_on in layered code.

## Custom

### CUSTOM-gate-is-mcp _(custom · domain: governance)_
The layer-1 governance gate is the Rust MCP tool-gateway. Gate logic is written against core::GovernanceGateway and MUST NOT assume Claude PreToolUse hooks. Verified feasible in Rust (docs/RUST_CORE_VERIFICATION.md).

### CUSTOM-core-zero-model-calls _(custom · domain: architecture)_
The orchestrator core makes ZERO LLM calls. All model interaction happens in the agent layer (camerata-agent), behind core::AgentDriver. Core is deterministic.

### CUSTOM-agents-locked-to-gate _(custom · domain: governance)_
Agents run via 'claude -p' with all built-in file-writing tools stripped (--disallowedTools) and tools restricted to the gated MCP server (--strict-mcp-config). There is no built-in escape from the gate.

### CUSTOM-seam-first _(custom · domain: architecture)_
Provider/runtime/transport concerns live behind the three core seams (GovernanceGateway, AgentDriver, CheckRunner). The core never assumes a specific model, provider, or MCP transport.
